title: ⚔️ Chapter boss ⭐⭐⭐: planning the day in London

questions.prompt: The sky is clear and sunny. Complete: "We ___ take our umbrellas to the park today."
questions.explanation: The negative of "be going to" puts "not" after are: aren't going to take. "Don't going to" uses the wrong auxiliary, "not going to" forgets the verb be, and "aren't go to" drops the -ing of going.

questions.prompt: Complete: "The guide says the weather in London is ___ in summer than in winter."
questions.explanation: "Good" is irregular: its comparative is "better" (than), not a form with -er or more. "Gooder", "more good" and "more better" are all impossible — you must learn good → better by heart.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Skander says the fish and chips by the river was ___ meal of the whole trip."
questions.explanation: "Good" is irregular, so its superlative is "the best" ✓. "The goodest" and "the most good" do not exist, and "the better" is a comparative, not the top of the group. The common trap is trying to build a regular form from "good".

questions.prompt: Complete the question: "___ you going to visit the Tower of London tomorrow?"
questions.explanation: A "be going to" question begins with am/is/are before the subject: Are you going to visit…? ✓ "Do" and "Does" belong to the present simple, and "Is" does not agree with "you". The trap is using "do" as if it were an ordinary verb question.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Oxford Street is ___ than Camden Market, but Camden is more fun."
questions.explanation: "Crowded" has two syllables and does not end in -y, so its comparative is "more crowded than" ✓. "Crowdeder" and "more crowdeder" are impossible, and "most crowded" is a superlative, not a comparison of two places.

questions.prompt: You are tired after walking. Which suggestion is written correctly?

options.text: Why don't we to take a taxi?
Why we don't take a taxi?
Why don't we taking a taxi?
Why don't we take a taxi?

questions.explanation: "Why don't we" is followed by the base verb: Why don't we take a taxi? ✓ Option a adds an extra "to", option b puts the words in the wrong order, and option c uses -ing instead of the base verb. The trap is treating it like "How about", which alone takes -ing.
